one_line_takeaway: CC-BOS shows that combining an 8-dimensional jailbreak strategy search space with a fruit-fly-inspired bio-optimizer over classical Chinese prompts drives black-box ASR to ~100% across six major LLMs while needing far fewer queries than prior black-box attacks, but the paper's own ablation attributes most of the gain to the strategy/optimization machinery rather than to the classical-Chinese language choice itself.
key_finding: On AdvBench (Table 1), CC-BOS achieves 100% ASR on all six target models (Gemini-2.5-flash, Claude-3.7, GPT-4o, Deepseek-Reasoner, Qwen3, Grok-3), with Avg.Score (harmfulness, 0-5 scale) ranging 3.14-4.88, versus the strongest baseline ICRT which reaches at most 98% ASR (on Grok-3) and as low as 40% ASR on Claude-3.7 (Avg.Score 1.60). AutoDAN-Turbo-R and CL-GSO trail further (e.g., CL-GSO 40% ASR on Claude-3.7, Avg.Score 1.86). On CLAS and StrongREJECT-small (Table 2), CC-BOS reaches ASR of 98-100% across all six models, versus ICRT's notably weaker and more variable results (e.g., ICRT only 21% ASR on Claude-3.7 for CLAS, 23.33% on StrongREJECT). Query efficiency (Table 3, AdvBench): CC-BOS needs only 1.12-2.38 average queries per successful jailbreak across models, versus e.g. CL-GSO's 1.24-21.42 and TAP's 53.96-93.48. Against the Llama-Guard-3-8B input+output defense (Table 4), CC-BOS still attains 22-40% ASR (e.g., 40% on Claude-3.7) versus ICRT's 0-2% and GPTFUZZER's 0-16% under the same defense, though all methods' ASR collapses substantially relative to the no-defense condition (CC-BOS drops from 100% to 22-40%). Cross-model transferability (Table 5) shows adversarial prompts generated on one source model transfer to other targets at 76-96% ASR (e.g., Qwen3-sourced prompts reach 96% ASR on Grok-3). The ablation (Table 6) shows ASR on Claude-3.7/AdvBench rising from 18% (classical Chinese context alone) to 60% (+ multidimensional strategy) to 100% (+ bio-inspired optimization), and the translation module in evaluation raising measured ASR from 90% to 100%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CC-BOS, a black-box jailbreak framework that (1) is the first to use classical Chinese (a formal, semantically-compressed, metaphor-rich ancient written register, distinct from modern Chinese) as the linguistic medium for adversarial prompt generation; (2) formalizes jailbreak prompt construction as search over an 8-dimensional discrete strategy space (Role Identity, Behavioral Guidance, Mechanism, Metaphor Mapping, Expression Style, Knowledge Relation, Contextual Setting, Trigger Pattern), each dimension a Cartesian-product factor, so a candidate prompt is a tuple s=(s1,...,s8); (3) optimizes over this space with a Fruit Fly Optimization Algorithm (FOA)-based bio-inspired search that combines a 'smell search' local perturbation operator (with an exponentially decaying step bound), a 'vision search' operator that pulls dimensions toward the current global-best strategy with an increasing attraction probability schedule, and a cauchy-distribution mutation operator triggered on stagnation to escape local optima, plus hash-based deduplication of visited strategy tuples and an early-stopping threshold on fitness; and (4) adds a two-stage classical-Chinese-to-English translation module applied to model responses before scoring, to remove translation/interpretation bias from the consistency and keyword evaluators. Fitness combines a GPT-4o-based judge compliance score (0-100, scaled from a 0-5 rating) and a binary rejection-keyword heuristic (0 or 20), summed to a 0-120 range.
threat_model: Black-box jailbreak attacker with only query access (no gradients/logits/weights) to a target LLM. The attacker uses a separate 'attack LLM' (Deepseek-Chat, used for both prompt generation/mutation guidance and translation) to generate classical-Chinese adversarial prompts from an 8D strategy vector and an original harmful query, submits them to the target model, and iteratively refines the strategy using the target's responses as fitness signal via the bio-inspired search loop. The paper also evaluates against an external safety filter (Llama-Guard-3-8B) applied to input and/or output as a defense-aware black-box setting.
limitations: The paper's own ablation shows only 18% ASR from the classical-Chinese framing alone, meaning most of the reported gain (18%->100%) is attributable to the strategy-space design and optimization procedure rather than to classical Chinese per se, which the abstract's framing risks overstating. The judge-model-based ASR/Avg.Score evaluation depends on a GPT-4o judge and on the paper's own translation module, both potential sources of evaluation bias/circularity (the paper mitigates this partly via manual review, but the manual review's scale/agreement statistics are not reported in the visible text). Defense evaluation is limited to a single guard model (Llama-Guard-3-8B) rather than a broader set of production or state-of-the-art safety filters, and the paper does not report false-positive/benign-utility costs of the defense at the settings tested. All target models are closed/hosted APIs (per the Ethics Statement, 'closed-source victim model'), so results may not generalize to differently-trained open models beyond the one Qwen3 checkpoint tested. Attack budget was very small (population 5, max 5 iterations), which favors the method's own design but leaves unclear how baselines would perform under a matched, tuned budget versus their original recommended settings ('consistent with the original research').

datasets_used: AdvBench Harmful Behavior subset (520 total requests; deduplicated to 50 representative requests for evaluation, following prior work)
CLAS 2024 (Competition for LLM and Agent Safety) dataset (100 harmful queries)
quantitative_results: AdvBench ASR (CC-BOS): 100% on all six models (Gemini-2.5-flash, Claude-3.7, GPT-4o, Deepseek-Reasoner, Qwen3, Grok-3)
CLAS ASR (CC-BOS vs ICRT): 100 vs 96 (Gemini), 99 vs 21 (Claude-3.7), 99 vs 83 (GPT-4o), 99 vs 89 (Deepseek-Reasoner), 99 vs 86 (Qwen3), 100 vs 94 (Grok-3)
StrongREJECT ASR (CC-BOS vs ICRT): 98.30 vs 83.33 (Gemini), 98.30 vs 23.33 (Claude-3.7), 100 vs 71.67 (GPT-4o), 98.30 vs 76.67 (Deepseek-Reasoner), 98.30 vs 66.67 (Qwen3), 98.30 vs 93.33 (Grok-3)
Avg.Q (queries to reach success, CC-BOS): 1.46 (Gemini), 2.38 (Claude-3.7), 1.28 (GPT-4o), 1.12 (Deepseek-Reasoner), 1.54 (Qwen3), 1.18 (Grok-3), versus CL-GSO's 3.62-21.42 and TAP's 53.96-93.48

Llama-Guard-3-8B defense, no-defense vs input+output filtering ASR: Claude-3.7 GPTFUZZER 0.00% -> 0.00%, ICRT 40% -> 26%, CC-BOS 100% -> 40%; Deepseek-Reasoner CC-BOS 100% -> 28%; Gemini-2.5-flash CC-BOS 100% -> 22%
Cross-model transferability ASR range: 76% to 96% (e.g., Qwen3-sourced prompts: 96% ASR on Grok-3, 90% on Gemini-2.5-flash)
Ablation on Claude-3.7/AdvBench: Classical Chinese alone 18% ASR; + multidimensional strategy 60% ASR; + bio-inspired optimization (full CC-BOS) 100% ASR
Evaluation-pipeline ablation: ASR measured without translation module 90%, with translation module 100%
